products.description: Just down the hill from my home, across Highway 522 in Woodinville. The former 76 gas station has been abandoned for many years despite being poised at the entrance to downtown. The midcentury angles contrast sharply with the graffiti.

products.title: Alone 2023
products.description: Clyde, WA

products.title: Birches 2015
products.description: Snowshoeing in the foothills outside Santa Fe, New Mexico, on a windy, cold winter day. I didn’t have the right coat, but I had my camera. Office visitors have often asked for a copy as a screensaver.

products.title: Coupeville 2023
products.description: We have a family tradition to stay for a long weekend in Coupeville, Washington on Penn Cove each October. Coupeville Wharf is an icon, and the sky was especially moody on this blustery early morning.

products.title: Deception 2026
products.description: I used aerial cameras for this, flying in from several miles away over Deception Pass at the North tip of Whidbey Island, Washington. I was thrilled to turn the camera down and capture the 1935 bridge that floats almost two hundred feet above a silvery spring afternoon.

products.title: Ebey 2024
products.description: I was fortunate to discover Ebey’s Landing on Whidbey Island on my first trip there, and it remains a beautiful place to observe the Salish Sea, from the beach or the bluffs. This was a magnificent autumn day – windy and warmer than it looks.

products.title: Labor Day 2002
products.description: Summer’s end. This was shot through my windshield during a fierce Atlantic downpour on the ferry dock at Orient Point, New York heading back to Massachusetts on Labor Day.

products.title: Lake 2022
products.description: Lake Twentytwo in ’22, actually—on the northern shoulder of Mount Pilchuck in the Cascade Range. Still plenty of snowpack on the way up to the outflow. The brilliant contrast of the clouds reminds me of the booming snowmelt and icefalls, cracking and echoing from the cliff face.

products.title: Merry 2011
products.description: I have no idea why this happened - a solo chimney, house long gone, still decorated for the holidays, on the road up to Yosemite National Park in December.

products.title: Modern 2025
products.description: Aquebogue, New York, close to where I grew up, home to the Modern Snack Bar since 1950. I shot its iconic mid-century sign standing in the middle of Main Road, dressed in black—possibly not the best attire given the speeding October traffic.

products.title: Palm Springs 2022
products.description: During an architecture tour of desert homes in the Twin Palms neighborhood of Palm Springs, one retro Atomic Age home had a fantastic collection of midcentury cars, including this 1958 Oldsmobile Super 88 in “Turquoise Mist”.

products.title: Pike 2025
products.description: Leaving an art walk in the Belltown neighborhood of Seattle, I was struck by the momentary emptiness of Pike Place, angular neon jutting in different lines and angles.

products.title: Reading 2024
products.description: Vik, Iceland

products.title: Scholastic 2009
products.description: Tallgrass Prairie National Preserve, northeast of Wichita, Kansas, is the largest remaining stand of what the great prairies of the Midwest were before farms, cities, and towns. The Lower Fox Creek School opened in 1884, closed in 1930, and—restored—was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1974.

products.title: Sequim 2022
products.description: In August 2022, we spent several days staying in Sequim, Washington on the Olympic Peninsula while visiting Olympic National Park. Sequim is one of the sunniest spots on the Pacific Northwest - outside the “rain shadow” of the mountains and known for it’s lavender fields and ocean views. This station sits just east of downtown. And since my father worked for Texaco for almost twenty years, it was an obvious subject.

products.title: Silver Lightning 2015
products.description: At the Best of the Northwest 2025, a friend asked if I’d considered wokring with Birches 2015 in black and white. It was a great idea – the trees are radiant against that high New Mexico winter desert sky.

products.title: Stark 2023
products.description: An editing “accident” while proofing Alone 2023—the same original image—led me to appreciate the great tonal contrasts of this abandoned house in Clyde, Washington.

products.title: Taos 2023
products.description: Taos Pueblo—a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a U.S. National Landmark—was built between 1000–1450 AD. Although 160 people still live in the complex, it was a surprisingly long wait to find a moment without tourists in Orlando Magic jerseys wandering into the shot.

products.title: Tire Center 2025
products.description: Another image from the abandoned 76 gas station in Woodinville, Washington. Just as 20th Century America saw the world through television screens, today we increasingly view our world through cell phones.

products.title: Town Beach 1986
products.description: The largest beach on Long Island Sound in Southold, New York on the North Fork. I lifeguarded at many beaches, including this one, when I was growing up.

products.title: Trulock's 2020
products.description: In August 2020, I was driving cross country solo. Midafternoon, I pulled off to find gas - and found this station instead. It reminded me of Edward Hopper’s famous “Gas” at the Museum of Modern Art in New York. Also, my father worked for Texaco for almost twenty years.

products.title: Wharf 2026
products.description: Aerial photography allows you to “rehearse” a route once and then use autopilot to repeat the circle. Of all the angles, I love seeing the 1905 Coupeville Town Wharf in front of the town it symbolizes. And for my friends back in New England, this is Whidbey Island, Washington, not Maine.

products.title: Woodhenge 2025
products.description: I’ve taken a few pictures along Chuckanut Drive overlooking Samish Bay and the San Juan Islands. This improvised trilithon, built by an unknown visitor in August, framed the idea of Chuckanut—the trees, the rock face, the Salish Sea.

products.title: Wreck 2025
products.description: Shot on an extremely dark late October morning near the mouth of the Samish River, near Edison, Washington, I was drawn to the long-abandoned wreck of a wooden boat, its owners and journeys lost to time. This image is printed on ChromaLuxe gloss white aluminum. Fabrication and shipping takes 10-14 days. Shipping is included.

posts.title: My First Time at the Bellevue Arts Fair
posts.description: There are certain art festivals that quietly sit on a photographer’s horizon—events you hear about, visit as an attendee, and file away as a future milestone. For me, the Bellevue Arts Fair  has long been one of those. In 2026, I’ll be exhibiting there for the first time, and it feels like a meaningful moment in the ongoing life of my work. This will also be my first appearance at the fair under Cascadia Oceanic , my landscape photography studio. Over the past year, the work has continued to...
posts.bodyHtml: There are certain art festivals that quietly sit on a photographer’s horizon—events you hear about, visit as an attendee, and file away as a future milestone. For me, the Bellevue Arts Fair  has long been one of those. In 2026, I’ll be exhibiting there for the first time, and it feels like a meaningful moment in the ongoing life of my work. This will also be my first appearance at the fair under Cascadia Oceanic , my landscape photography studio. Over the past year, the work has continued to...
posts.author: Chris McNulty

posts.title: Woodinville May Art Walk: New Work at Prohibition Cellars
posts.description: One of the things I love most about spring in Woodinville is how naturally art, wine, and community come together. The May Art Walk  is a perfect example of that—and this year, I’m excited to be sharing new photographic work  at Prohibition Cellars . For this event, I’ll be showing a selection of recently completed images, including work that hasn’t been displayed publicly before. Seeing photographs online is one thing, but experiencing them in person—at scale, with texture and detail—is...
posts.bodyHtml: One of the things I love most about spring in Woodinville is how naturally art, wine, and community come together. The May Art Walk  is a perfect example of that—and this year, I’m excited to be sharing new photographic work  at Prohibition Cellars . For this event, I’ll be showing a selection of recently completed images, including work that hasn’t been displayed publicly before. Seeing photographs online is one thing, but experiencing them in person—at scale, with texture and detail—is...
posts.author: Chris McNulty

posts.title: Looking Ahead: A Season of Making and Sharing
posts.description: Preview of new photos and my upcoming 2026 shows.
posts.bodyHtml: Preview of new photos and my upcoming 2026 shows.
posts.author: Chris McNulty

posts.title: That's a Wrap - Best of the NW 2025
posts.description: Good morning! It’s been a whirlwind couple of days at Magnuson Park for my first-ever public exhibition at the Best of the Northwest 2025 Fall Show . I was both honored and humbled to meet so many enthusiasts who stopped by my booth. They focused deeply on my images and shared their thoughts and reactions. I also want to give a huge shoutout to my friends and family who came out to support the event. A special thanks to Michelle, my tireless and fearless co-creator and curator of our art and...
posts.bodyHtml: Good morning! It’s been a whirlwind couple of days at Magnuson Park for my first-ever public exhibition at the Best of the Northwest 2025 Fall Show . I was both honored and humbled to meet so many enthusiasts who stopped by my booth. They focused deeply on my images and shared their thoughts and reactions. I also want to give a huge shoutout to my friends and family who came out to support the event. A special thanks to Michelle, my tireless and fearless co-creator and curator of our art and...
posts.author: Chris McNulty

posts.title: Exciting News: My First Photography Show This Fall!
posts.description: Please join me November 7 through 9 at the Best of the Northwest Art Show, held in the stunning Magnuson Park in Seattle, Washington.
posts.bodyHtml: Please join me November 7 through 9 at the Best of the Northwest Art Show, held in the stunning Magnuson Park in Seattle, Washington.
posts.author: Chris McNulty

posts.title: Curating with Code: Crowdsourcing Photography
posts.description: Chris McNulty invites the public to help curate his Nov 7 art show via a custom-built photo voting app. Users compare image pairs, vote without logging in, and influence the final gallery. The app offers contests, leaderboards, mobile/PWA features, and integrated print purchases. Admin tools include bulk uploads, analytics, and contest management—blending tech and community for collaborative curation
posts.bodyHtml: Chris McNulty invites the public to help curate his Nov 7 art show via a custom-built photo voting app. Users compare image pairs, vote without logging in, and influence the final gallery. The app offers contests, leaderboards, mobile/PWA features, and integrated print purchases. Admin tools include bulk uploads, analytics, and contest management—blending tech and community for collaborative curation

posts.title: Exciting News: My Photography Debut at the Best of the Northwest Art Show
posts.description: Get ready for my official launch party and the debut of my online store, Cascadia Oceanic, in October 2025.

posts.title: New York City
posts.description: The great city - largest in the USA, is too vast to cover in one blog poist.  No one even knows how many restaurants they have. I was...

events.description: Showing recent work at Prohibition Cellars during the May Art Walk. A perfect spring afternoon of art, wine, and community.

events.title: Bellevue Arts Fair 2026
events.description: First-time exhibitor at one of the Pacific Northwest's most beloved art festivals. Visit my booth for landscape and seascape work, including new pieces from 2026.
